coredata.dc:title: Waste derivitized blue luminescent carbon quantum dots for selenite sensing in water
coredata.dc:creator: Devi, Pooja
Kaur, Gurvinder
Thakur, Anupma
Kaur, Navneet
Grewal, Anita
Kumar, Praveen
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report an environmental friendly, facile, and completely green synthetic method for producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from whey, a major dairy waste. The as-prepared monodispersed diameter CQDs exhibit blue luminescence with noteworthy quantum yield (~11.4%) and excitation dependent emission behaviour. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis reveals the presence of aromatized carbon peaks, leading to polymerized CQDs diameter architecture during whey pyrolysis. The X-ray and selected area electron diffraction patterns confirm their amorphous nature. Further, we demonstrate, these CQDs as an effective sensor probe for selective selenite monitoring in water upon functionalization with appropriate ligand. The functionalized GCQDs probe is shown to detect selenite with high sensitivity in 10–1000ppb detection range. Further it is selective for selenite over other relevant ions (such as Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Se6+, Cl-, Br-, NO3 −, NO2 − and F−) and displays a sub-ppb detection limit at 1.1% relative standard deviation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Diary waste
Photoluminescence
Sensor
Selenium

(inset, Fig. 1(b): SAED and lattice fringe analysis of as synthesized GCQDs). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Absorption & emission Characteristic of GCQDs and (b) effect of excitation wavelength on emission behaviour. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
: (a) Effect of time and (b) temperature on optical characteristic of GCQDs.
: Quantum yield of GCQDs synthesized at different temperatures.
: Vibrational characteristics of GCQDs and fGCQDs.
: Sensing behaviour of fGCQDs towards selenite.
(a) Analytical performance of fGCQDs towards selenite in 10 to 1000ppb concentration range (b) regression fit for relation between fluorescence intensity (F/Fo) and selenite concentration.
: Stern-Volmer's plot with varied concentration of selenite.
Effect of 100ppb interfering ions on the relative fluorescence intensity of fGCQDs.

head.ce:title: Waste derivitized blue luminescent carbon quantum dots for selenite sensing in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, New Delhi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Council of Scientific and Industrial Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Chandigarh 160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Institute of Engineering & Technology, Kurukshetra University, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University Institute of Engineering & Technology, Kurukshetra University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report an environmental friendly, facile, and completely green synthetic method for producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from whey, a major dairy waste. The as-prepared monodispersed diameter CQDs exhibit blue luminescence with noteworthy quantum yield (~11.4%) and excitation dependent emission behaviour. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis reveals the presence of aromatized carbon peaks, leading to polymerized CQDs diameter architecture during whey pyrolysis. The X-ray and selected area electron diffraction patterns confirm their amorphous nature. Further, we demonstrate, these CQDs as an effective sensor probe for selective selenite monitoring in water upon functionalization with appropriate ligand. The functionalized GCQDs probe is shown to detect selenite with high sensitivity in 10–1000ppb detection range. Further it is selective for selenite over other relevant ions (such as Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Se6+, Cl-, Br-, NO3 −, NO2 − and F−) and displays a sub-ppb detection limit at 1.1% relative standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have recently up-surged as dominant light emitting candidate in wide physiochemical applications such as photo-catalysis, bio-sensing, drug delivery, chemical sensing, bio-imaging, electro-catalysis, and so forth [1,2]. Their low toxicity, biocompatibility, environment friendliness, photo stability, easy functionalization and tunable fluorescence characteristics make these CQDs advantageous over the conventional semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) in nanotechnology sphere [3–5]. Of late, they have been extensively explored for diverse applications towards sensing (chemo/bio) and energy harvesting. In bio sensing, they are well accepted probes for rapid detection of alkaline phosphatase in biological samples [6], ultrasensitive detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA) towards real-world diagnostic applications [7], as a FRET probe for bleomycin detection [8], etc. Besides, they are known to enhance photoelectrochemical water oxidation in view of their catalytic role as well as an sensitizer [9]. Therefore, developing effective strategies to prepare CQDs is of significance in recent scenario. To date, CQDs are generally synthesized by laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, ultrasonication, heat-treatment-based strategy such as refluxing, microwave, or solvothermal heating, etc., engaging expensive chemical precursors, special equipment, critical synthesis conditions and post treatment processes [10,11], which limit their use for wide applications. Thus, developing simple and low-cost methods for rapid production of CQDs is highly desirable to use their full potential. In contrast, natural precursors have emerged out as possible nano-factories for their large scale and inexpensive production via green synthesis, which eliminates the use of toxic materials and harsh reaction conditions [12]. Earlier, CQDs synthesis have been reported from egg white [13], milk [14], banana juice [15], honey [16], soy milk [2], garlic [17], coconut husk [18], orange juice [19], cabbage [12], pomelo peel [20], bamboo leaves [21], grass [22], rice straw [23], strawberry juice [24], sweet potatoes [25] and sugarcane juice [26], which are of some or other use in our day-to-day life. To the best of our knowledge, however, no attention has been paid to producing CQDs from whey, which is a major dairy & cheese industry waste product. It constitutes watery portion after the separation of fat and caseins from whole milk, and causes severe environmental problem in the surrounding when discharged in natural resources due to its high lactose content, which leads to enormous biochemical oxygen demand [27]. In this communication, we demonstrate a facile, low cost, and green preparative strategy towards water-soluble, carbon-rich and photoluminescent CQDs by pyrolysis of whey at high temperature for the first time. Approximately, 1kg cheese production generates 9kg of whey as waste product, which is of no use [28]. Therefore, to tackle the problem of its discharge to protect environment, and also to provide a low cost and alternate resource for CQDs, we investigated it as a possible precursor for synthesis of this highly valued material via green route.

Further, these green synthesized CQDs (GCQDs) are also demonstrated for selective sensing application towards selenite (Se(IV)), which is an emerging water pollutant worldwide, as recommended by World Health Organization (WHO) on intake above10 ppb [29]. On over supplementation, it is reported to cause toxicity and oxidative stress. The replacement of sulfur with selenium in hair and keratin [30], prominently leads to structural defects, brittle hair, thickened & stratified nails and pulmonary oedema causing multiple organ disorder called selenosis. The chronic selenosis is chiefly associated with continuous and long term ingestion of selenite contaminated drinking water [31]. The existing optical probes are mostly organic fluorophores based, which suffer from bleaching issues (Fig S1). Recently, semiconductor quantum dots are also reported for selenite quantification in water, however, they are based upon heavy metals/metalloids and could cause toxicity to the environment. The presented probe, however, is environmental friendly and exhibit excellent analytical characteristic for real time application of selenite monitoring in water.

Whey, carbon source for CQDs, was purchased from supermarket and stored in refrigerator at 4°C, when not used. Quinine sulfate (QS, 99.5%, AR) used for quantum yield measurements was supplied by CDH, India. Standard stock solutions (1000ppm) of Se and various ions (As5+, As3+, Se6+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, Br−, and F−) were purchased from Inorganic Venture. Ultrapure deionized (DI) water obtained from Millipore (Resistance: 18 MΏ) was used for preparing reagents and to perform sensing study.

The synthesis of GCQDs by means of pyrolysis was done with hot air oven (Simtronics, SIM 9075) and observed at first for the fluorescence in UV cabinet (Popular India). Optical characteristics of GCQDs were probed by UV–visible (Hitachi U-3900-H), photoluminescence (PL, Carry Varian) and FT-IR (670 IR Varian) spectrophotometer. Raman spectra was recorded using inVia Raman (Renishaw) spectrophotometer using laser (λex=785nm). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 STWIN) were used to determine microstructure and size of the GCQDs. XRD patterns were recorded by X-ray diffractometer (Xpert-Pro). NMR of as synthesized GCQDs was recorded with 400MHz Avance-II spectrometer (Bruker Corporation).

The collected whey from diary market was used as such without any purification and subjected to heat treatment in hot air oven for few minutes. The resultant dark brown product was diluted with DI water (20mL) and filtered through whatmann filter paper to obtain filtrate containing GCQDs. The effect of temperature and time on optical characteristics of GCQDs was studied for 180–225°C temperature and 10–40mins time range. The quantum yield of as synthesized GCQDs was calculated using QS (in 0.1MH2SO4, quantum yield 0.54 at 360nm)) as reference and measured according to established procedure [12]. To develop GCQDs-ligand interface for Se (IV), as synthesized GCQDs were first esterified to methyl ester in the presence of an acid followed by their overnight dialysis and thereafter functionalization with amine rich ligand, earlier synthesized by our group. The resultant product, functionalized GCQDs (fGCQDs) were further purified by dialysis overnight.

To demonstrate sensing capability of GCQDs with modified surface, the sensing study was done with PL spectroscopy using different concentrations of Se (IV) (10–100 ppb) prepared from stock solution. For selectivity study, various interferents (As5+, As3+, Se6+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, Br−, and F−) were studied at 10 fold concentration.

The GCQDs were synthesized by one step pyrolysis of raw whey, which contains lactose as a major sugar content, turned to light yellow color after heat treatment at 220°C in oven. The synthesis of fluorescent CQDs might have involved incomplete carbonization of lactose at high temperature, since the lactose content of acid whey is more than citric acid [32]. The particulate character of the product is identified by SEM & TEM and same is portrayed in Fig. 1(a) & (b), respectively, depicting spherical, uniformly distributed CQDs with an average diameter of ~4nm. Selected area electron diffraction analysis (SAED), Fig. 1(b) inset, reveals their amorphous nature (lattice fringe ~0.31nm) and is in agreement with previous reports. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) result is in line with the TEM result and Zeta potential was measured to be −8.3mV, which can be attributed to the presence of the carboxyl groups on their surface [ESI, Fig. S1]. Further, hydrodynamic diameters indicated the favorable dispersibility of GCQDs in aqueous solution for long term and can be attributed to electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged GCQDs resulting in electro steric stabilization [33]. The appearance of a broad diffraction peak in XRD spectrum centered at 22.7° as shown in Fig. 1(c), further confirms their amorphous nature due to presence of highly disordered sp3 bonded carbon atoms [34]. The Raman spectrum of GCQDs (Fig. 1(d)) clearly specifies sharp D and G peaks, at ~1368 and 1661cm−1, confirming their synthesis from whey by supporting the hybridization state of carbon. Further, the presence of broad D-band and sharp G-band corresponding to defects and E2g mode, respectively, indicates the presence of sp2 graphitic defect sites in amorphous carbon. The high ratio of ID/IG bands implies presence of out of plane vibrations indicating the presence of structural defects in synthesized GCQDs [4]. In solid state 13C NMR spectrum (DMSO-d
6 , 100MHz, ppm) of the GCQDs [ESI, Fig. S2], several peaks indicative of sp2 carbon atoms, attributed to methyl groups (CH3) (32.3–52.6ppm), aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms [35] & alkenes (CC) (135.34–139.42ppm), O-bonded aliphatic (72.21–78.31ppm), carbon-aromatic (127.11–129.47ppm), and carboxyl carbons (169.05–174.54ppm) were observed [36]. It implies that the synthesized GCQDs structure has common aromatized carbon peaks that led to aromatic chains polymerization resulting in the formation of dot like architecture as observed for other carbon nano-dot structures [37].

The absorption and emission characteristics of aqueous dispersion of GCQDs are shown in Fig. 2. Raw whey doesn’t exhibit any characteristic absorption peak, however, after pyrolysis of same, a sharp peak centered around ~280nm was observed, which could be chalked to Π-Π* transition of the carbon present in an aromatic system with π orbitals in GCQD [14]. Whereas, the PL emission of GCQDs was centered at 425nm in 350–600nm range on excitation at 350nm, indicating that the GCQDs are fluorescent in nature, which was not the case with raw whey. The photograph of light yellowed colored dispersion under UV light exhibit a blue color (inset, Fig. 2(a)), further revealing that the resultant GCQDs exhibit blue fluorescence. On excitation at different excitation wavelengths, the increase in PL intensity and shift of emission peaks at longer wavelengths was observed as shown in Fig. 2(b), which confirms its excitation dependent emission behaviour and is in agreement with previous reports utilized for multicolor imaging applications [18]. The observed excitation dependent-emission behaviour can be assigned to multiple emission centers in synthesized GCQDs [38].

Fig. 3 portrays the effect of synthesis time and temperature on optical properties of the GCQDs. The PL emission pattern at different synthesis time shows maximum intensity at 30mins treatment, however, on further increase, PL intensity significantly decreased, which can be related to the burning of raw material at higher temperature instead of pyrolysis. The observed behaviour is in line of the report published by Chunduri et al. on effect of synthesis parameters on optical properties of CQDs [18]. Therefore, an optimized synthesis time of 30min was chosen for temperature study. As reaction temperature increases from 180°C to 220°C, PL intensity increases monotonically, and thereafter abrupt decrease in intensity was observed, hence 220°C was selected as optimal temperature for further synthesis of GCQDs with the maximum quantum yield of ~11.4% calculated with reference to QS ( Fig. 4). The observed quantum yield is comparable to existing GCQDs synthesized from various other liquid precursors. Moreover, the presented approach is less time consuming, very facile, cost effective and utilizes waste material as a precursor in comparison to existing green synthesis reports for CQDs [ESI, Table S1]. The effect of excitation wavelength on emission characteristics of GCQDs synthesized at different temperatures was also studied, which also showed excitation dependent behaviour. Further, the electrochemical characterization revealed the enhanced current characteristics with GCQDs modified glassy carbon electrode, which shows their potential for design of an electrochemical sensor as well as electrocatalytic materials (data not provided). However, in the present work, we have demonstrated them as an optical probe after functionalization with amine rich ligand.

The functionality of GCQDs synthesized from whey was probed by FT-IR and presented in Fig. 5. The observed broad peak between 3200 and 3500cm−1 are assigned to O-H vibrational stretching indicating the presence of residual hydroxyl groups on its surface. The sharp peak at ~1639cm−1 is assigned of the presence of -CO vibrational stretching reveals the existence of a carboxylate. The absorption peak at ~2348 and 2153cm−1 can be ascribed to OCO and CC, which shows the evolution of carbon dioxide gas and presence of alkyne group, respectively. In addition, the peaks at ~ 1945cm−1 and 670cm−1 corresponds to the C-H vibrations of methyl or methylene. It manifests that synthesized GCQDs contain characteristic functional groups including –OH, -COOH on surface facilitating their potential biological application in conjugating drug or target moieties and impart them with excellent water solubility without any further requirement of chemical modification [39]. Whereas after functionalization, fGCQD, showed a noticeable difference in FT-IR characteristics from GCQDs, by appearance of new vibrational band at 1670cm−1 and 3420cm−1 , which are assigned to carbonyl amide functional moieties and aromatic amine (–NH) functional groups, respectively [40]. The presence of these groups on fGCQDs surface suggests the amine rich ligand is chemically grafted onto the surface of GCQDs.

After imparting selectivity to GCQDs synthesized from whey, they were investigated against selenite (Se (IV)), which is one of the major emerging water pollutant. Fig. 6 shows that drastic decrease in PL intensity of fGCQDs on addition of 1000 ppb Se (IV), which implies the surface moieties of these dots are capable to detect Se(IV) species. The possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching corresponds to elimination of the vacancies by means of complex formation between amine groups and Se (IV), thereby facilitating non-radiative e−/h+ recombination on the fGCQDs surface. This behaviour is in agreement with previous CQDs quenching results in the literature [41].

Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence response of fGCQDs was studied towards a concentration range of selenite from 10 to 1000 ppb and regression curves,is shown in Fig. 7(a) and (b). A fashioned decrease in fGCQDs intensity can be seen on addition of increasing concentration of selenite. The fGCQDs probe could detect as low as 10 ppb Se (IV), which completely meets the requirement of routine selenium analysis in potable water. The association constant of the Se-fGCQDs complex was calculated using fluorescence quenching data by Benesi-Hildebrand method [ESI, Fig. S3]:
1
(
F
O
−
F
)
=
1
(
F
O
−
F
b
)
+
1
K
(
F
O
−
F
b
)
[
Se
(
IV
)
]
where K is the apparent association constant, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of fGCQDs (in the absence of Se(IV)), Fb is the fluorescence intensity of fGCQDs when they are quenched by with excess of Se(IV) and F is the observed fluorescence intensity of fGCQDs in the presence of varied amounts of Se(IV). The value of K was obtained from a plot of 1/(F0−F) against 1/[Se(IV)], where K is equal to the intercept/slope and is found to be 9.97×105 M−1. The high value of association constant implies high selectivity of fGCQDs probe towards selenite in view of high dissociation energy between N-Se complexes (381kJ/mol), which is well utilized concept for selective selenium analysis [42].

However, a model is proposed to fit observed actual behaviour in 10–100ppb concentration range and fits best onto the triplicate runs towards selenite sensing is shown in Fig. 8(a). Furthermore, predicted values are nearly in line with the experimental results obtained for respective selenite concentration. The proposed model equation is:
y =
108
1 +
e
8 ( x − 0 . 67 )
where, y is selenite concentration and x is F/Fo. The predicted values of concentration using the proposed equation was validated with various experimental values and RMSE is calculated to be 1.654 [ESI, Table S2]. The theoretical detection limit (LOD) of the probe towards selenite was estimated at a signal to noise ratio of 3 and fitting parameter of 0.98 using Stern-Volmer plot for linear equation. Under optimized conditions, the luminescence quenching caused by analyte (Se (IV)) is well described by the Stern−Volmer equation:
I
O
I
=
K
SV
(
Q
)
+
C
where I0 and I are the luminescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher (analyte i.e. Se(IV)), respectively, KSV is the Stern−Volmer constant representing the affinity between Luminopher (fGCQDs) and quencher (Se(IV)), Q is the analyte concentration, and C is a constant close to 1. The Stern−Volmer constant (Ksv), was calculated to be 8.48×105 M−1 in the 10−50μgL−1 range for a linear regression equation
I
o
I
=
8.48 ×
10
5
C + 0.9640
and is presented in Fig. 8(b). The precision of replicate measurements, expressed as a relative standard deviation (RSD, n=3), which 1.1% in our case, the theoretical LOD, which is defined as the analyte concentration equivalent to 3s (standard deviation) was calculated to be 0.035 ppb, which is well below the WHO guidelines of 10ppb. The limit of quantification, equivalent to 10s, was calculated to be 0.116ppb and precision of experiments for triplicates was calculated as a percentage using the ratio of standard deviation and means of replicates and is found to be 0.01%.

The analytical performance of fGCQDs in terms of selectivity was investigated against commonly found interfering ions such as Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Se6+, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, NO2 − and F− and is shown in Fig. 9. It can be seen very clearly that selenite induced almost 70% quenching which otherwise is not the case for other ionic species even at 10 fold concentrations, which ensures the selectivity of this platform. The slight quenching (11%) observed in case of Pb2+ could be assigned to possible H–Pb2+ complex formation, however was not very significant. Otherwise, it can also be taken care by using chelating agents for its removal prior to selenite analysis [43].

In consideration of high sensitivity and selectivity of fGCQDs probes, their practicality was validated against real water samples collected from lake, ground and well of various districts of Punjab, India. Before analysis, the collected water samples were filtered through a 0.22µm membrane to remove suspended particles and spiked with selenite standard solution to achieve 10ppb selenite concentration. It was observed that fluorescence intensity is enhanced on addition of 10ppb spiked water samples collected from various sources and the accuracy of the fGCQDs was determined by % recovery calculated with predicted values as shown in [ESI, Table S3]. The results showed that analytical recoveries of selenite added to water samples were ranged from 89% to 108%. This implies that despite of the presence of numerous minerals in real water samples, the proposed sensing platform can effectively distinguish between selenite contaminated and non-contaminated water samples to even 10 ppb level, satisfying the WHO (10ppb) & USEPA (50ppb) criterion of safe limit in drinking water. These experimental results show that the response of the designed fGCQDs is unaffected by other reported interfering ions in view of Se-N complex selectivity. Besides GCQDs have earlier been reported for detection of several other heavy metals ions such as Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+[ESI, Table S4]. However, the application of GCQDs towards selenite, an emerging water pollutant has not been explored yet and is of high relevance. In summary, the synthesized GCQDs from diary waste can further be explored for other metals ions and bio-sensing applications upon modification with appropriate ligand.

Whey, a major as well as hazardous diary waste, has been demonstrated as a precursor for the synthesis of CQDs by a facile approach without using complex instrumentation. Lactose, a major whey content, polymerization has been proposed as possible mechanism for their synthesis, as revealed from NMR analysis. TEM observation reveals mono-dispersed particulates with average size of 4nm, whereas XRD depicts their amorphous nature. Under optimized synthesis parameters, they exhibit blue luminescence and a comparable quantum yield of 11.4%. After functionalization with nitrogen rich ligands, confirmed by FTIR, these GCQDs have been used for selective selenite monitoring in water to sub ppb level at 1.1% RSD. These results are in line with waste to wealth approach, wherein the hazardous waste is converted to a sensory probe for hazardous metalloid (selenite) sensing in water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Director, CSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh for his kind permission to carry out this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.03.069.
